title: Restriction problem for non-generic representation of Arthur type
speaker: Wee Teck Gan
abstract: Abstract: The Gross-Prasad conjecture considers a branching problem for generic Arthur packets of classical groups. In this talk, we will describe progress towards extending this conjecture to nongeneric Arthur packets (this is joint work with Gross and Prasad). For GL(n), we describe some recent progress towards this conjecture by Max Gurevich.

proof_stages.label: Local branching problem and generic benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the Hom space for restricting irreducible smooth representations from GL(n+1) to GL(n), recalls multiplicity one, and reviews the generic nonvanishing theorem via Rankin-Selberg integrals in Whittaker models.

proof_stages.label: Arthur-type representations for GL(n)
proof_stages.summary: Arthur parameters are written as sums phi_i tensor S_{b_i}, and the corresponding GL(n) representation is built as an irreducible parabolic induction of generalized Speh representations, defining the nongeneric class to be studied.

proof_stages.label: Unitary restriction and SL2-type partition constraint
proof_stages.summary: The unitary version decomposes the restricted representation by a spectral measure, and results of Burger-Sarnak, Clozel, and Gross-Prasad/Li identify automorphic-dual support and an explicit map on partitions governing the SL2 type under restriction.

proof_stages.label: Smooth Arthur-parameter branching conjecture
proof_stages.summary: For pi of Arthur type on GL(n+1), the conjecture says that Arthur-type sigma on GL(n) occurs exactly when its parameter is obtained from that of pi by partitioning the blocks, replacing each b_i by b_i+1 or b_i-1, and filling any remaining dimension by an arbitrary tempered parameter.

proof_stages.label: Proved special cases
proof_stages.summary: The authors prove the conjecture in special cases, notably when the internal Deligne SL2 in the phi_i is absent and when there is only one Arthur block, i.e. a generalized Speh or ladder-type representation.

proof_stages.label: Mirabolic filtration and derivative reduction
proof_stages.summary: The proof begins by restricting to the mirabolic subgroup, using the Bernstein-Zelevinsky filtration to reduce nonzero branching to Hom spaces between derivatives, whose computation is manageable for one ladder but combinatorially difficult for products.

proof_stages.label: Gurevich's general necessity theorem via quantum affine input
proof_stages.summary: Max Gurevich proves the necessary direction in general, reducing the remaining combinatorics to Hom questions for products of truncated Speh representations and importing a theorem of Hernandez through affine Hecke algebras and quantum affine Schur-Weyl duality.

proof_stages.label: Classical-group extension and descent interpretation
proof_stages.summary: The same up/down recipe is proposed for Bessel models and Arthur packets of classical groups, with dual-pair and parity caveats, and it is shown to align with known automorphic descent constructions.

examples.name: Tempered and discrete-series specialization of the recipe
examples.note: When all Arthur-SL2 block sizes b_i are 1, the all-down choice recovers generic tempered descendants, and for an irreducible parameter this leaves only the generic quotient case, serving as a check against the known generic theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes the recipe to tempered parameters with all b_i=1, analyzes the I_-=I option, pumping-up alternatives, and the irreducible/discrete-series case.

passes.reason: Tempered/discrete-series case has all Arthur SL2 blocks of size 1, the minimal trivial-SL2 specialization of the recipe.
All Arthur-SL2 blocks have minimal size b_i=1, giving the tempered/discrete-series boundary case used as a check of the recipe.
all Arthur SL2 blocks have b_i=1, making this the trivial-SL2 tempered boundary/base case of the recipe
All Arthur SL2 blocks have minimal size b_i=1, with the discrete-series subcase a single-block boundary/base case used to check the recipe.
Tempered/discrete-series Arthur parameters have minimal SL2-block size b=1, so they are a boundary case of the recipe rather than a counterexample.
The tempered/discrete-series case has minimal Arthur SL2 blocks b_i=1 and is used as a sanity check of the recipe, not to refute it.
The tempered case has all Arthur SL2 block sizes b_i=1, a minimal/boundary Arthur-type specialization, and is used only to recover the known generic/discrete-series behavior.
The tempered/discrete-series case is used as a sanity-check specialization, and it lies on the minimal Arthur-SL2/tempered boundary of the recipe.
Tempered Arthur parameters have all SL2-block sizes b_i=1, the minimal/most-tempered boundary case, and are used to check the recipe recovers the generic/discrete-series situation.

The all-b_i=1 tempered case is the minimal Arthur-SL2 boundary used to recover the generic/discrete-series specialization, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Trivial representation of GL(n+1)
examples.note: The trivial representation has Arthur parameter 1 tensor S_{n+1}, and the recipe has only the down move to produce the trivial representation of GL(n), matching the obvious restriction.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes/checks the recipe for the trivial representation of GL(n+1), ruling out pumping up as too large and identifying the sole descendant as the trivial GL(n) representation.

passes.reason: The trivial GL(n+1) representation is a collapsed one-dimensional representation with the whole group acting trivially, used only as a consistency check.
The trivial GL(n+1) representation is one-dimensional with full group acting trivially and corresponds to the maximal single Arthur block.
the trivial GL(n+1) representation is one-dimensional and all-invariant, with a single Arthur block of maximal length
The trivial GL(n+1) representation is one-dimensional with trivial action, an endpoint Arthur parameter used only as a reality check.
The trivial GL(n+1) representation is one-dimensional with trivial action and corresponds to an endpoint single-block Arthur parameter.

The trivial GL(n+1) representation is one-dimensional and maximally collapsed/minimal, used as a reality check rather than to refute a claim.
The trivial GL(n+1) representation is one-dimensional and maximally invariant, used only to check that the recipe gives the trivial descendant.
The trivial representation of GL(n+1) is one-dimensional and maximally invariant/minimal, used only as a reality check for the restriction recipe.
The trivial GL(n+1) representation is one-dimensional and corresponds to the one-block Arthur endpoint, used only as a reality check.
The trivial representation of GL(n+1) is a collapsed one-dimensional representation with a single maximal Arthur block, used only as a reality check of the recipe.
The trivial GL(n+1) representation has collapsed one-dimensional group action and a maximal single Arthur block, used only as a reality check for the recipe.

examples.name: Trivial GL(n) representation and its possible ancestors
examples.note: Running the recipe backward for sigma trivial gives only the trivial GL(n+1) representation and a second family involving a tempered GL(2) parameter, illustrating how the conjecture predicts all Arthur-type pi with Hom(pi,sigma) nonzero.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works backward from the trivial GL(n) parameter to determine possible GL(n+1) ancestors, listing the trivial ancestor and a second family while excluding another candidate by dimension.

passes.reason: The central object is the trivial GL(n) representation, a one-dimensional fully invariant representation, with ordinary possible ancestors listed by the recipe.

examples.name: Finite Schur-Weyl model for the quantum affine dictionary
examples.note: By dropping the affine adjectives one sees the Weyl group S_n in duality with GL(n), an analogy used only to make the affine Hecke/quantum affine Schur-Weyl passage more concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The finite Schur-Weyl situation is invoked only as an analogy/dictionary for passing from Weyl groups to GL(n), without carrying out a concrete calculation on it.

passes.reason: The finite Schur-Weyl picture is a stripped-down non-affine base model for the quantum affine dictionary, not an adversarial example.
The finite Schur-Weyl case is the non-affine base specialization obtained by dropping the affine/quantum structure.
the finite Schur-Weyl picture uses the full symmetric-group/Weyl-group tensor-power duality as the non-affine base model for the quantum-affine dictionary
The finite Schur-Weyl analogy uses the full symmetric group acting regularly on tensor powers to motivate the quantum-affine dictionary.

The finite Schur-Weyl model is the highly regular symmetric-group/GL duality used as an analogy for the quantum affine dictionary.

The finite Schur-Weyl setting is used as a symmetric classical model for the affine quantum dictionary, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: SO(4n) to SO(2n+1) automorphic descent
examples.note: For the parameter phi tensor S_2 on SO(4n), the recipe forces descent to phi on SO(2n+1), explaining the output of the Ginzburg-Rallis-Soudry automorphic descent construction and its extensions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the recipe to the SO(4n) parameter φ⊗2, checks pumping up/down for descent to SO(2n+1), and connects the resulting parameter to the automorphic descent construction.

passes.reason: The SO(4n) to SO(2n+1) automorphic descent is a standard classical-family construction illustrating the recipe positively.
